summary: Joel Spolsky reflects on his company's office move and the lessons learned from managing a construction project. He warns against accepting lowball bids from contractors, explaining that unusually low bids signal incompetence, corner-cutting, or plans to overcharge on changes. He then challenges the common software industry claim that construction is a mature field with predictable schedules and budgets, arguing from firsthand experience that construction is just as unpredictable. He teases a future writeup about their architect's design for an ideal programmer workspace.
key_points: Lowball contractor bids are almost always a trap — the contractor either doesn't understand the work, is cutting corners, or plans to make up profit on change orders
The best use of a lowball bid is as leverage to negotiate a better price from your preferred contractor
The software industry's belief that construction is a mature field with predictable schedules and budgets is hogwash — construction has the same problems
Joel's office was designed by an architect to be the 'ultimate programmer's workspace'
money_quotes: When you bid out a construction job, there's always one guy who comes in with a bid that's way less than the other guys. It's almost always a bad idea to accept this bid.
Anyone whose bid is that far off either doesn't know what he's doing, or is cutting corners in some scary way, or will go bankrupt in the middle of the job.
You can buy an awful lot of aspirin for $35,000.
As a client, the best way to use a lowball bid is to get the contractor you really want to lower their bid.
What I've learned from my first large construction project is that this is hogwash. The building industry doesn't know how to do anything on schedule or on budget, either.
key_insight: The software industry's idealization of construction as a mature discipline with predictable schedules is a myth — construction projects are just as prone to budget and schedule overruns as software projects.
